speaker: interviewer
text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling. The data is November 14, 1996. We are interviewing the survivor, George Steinberg. We are in Longmeadow, Mass, USA. And continue, Donna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Steinberg--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of a growing sense of danger in the beginning of World War II leading up to World War II?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: How? How were you aware?

speaker: interviewee
text: How? When the war started, and the first, the Russians started to  bomb Poland. And I had already in that time a horse and wagon. And at that time, I was already, and I took a [INAUDIBLE]. And we put it in to go away somewhere. And we went four or five kilometers. And we saw already how we looked-- to be a refugee or to run and come back to them. And then the Russians came in, in a couple of days, two days. And the Russians came, and-- As a matter of fact, when the Russians came in, I find a job. I was still young and lucky, because the Poles didn't take me away to the army, which I mentioned to you before, they took away from [INAUDIBLE]. But my luck-- I had luck in my life. And they didn't call me. I was ready with a bag to go because they took my friends, other ones. And then I was home. And then the Russians came in. And I was still young, you know, to go even the Russians took it in, because they took away one of my brothers. And he didn't come back. It was later where he was killed. And I was luckier than them. And then I was capable in my way, in my life a little bit, and they didn't take me to the army in Russian. They took me. And I found a job for-- was not too far from us, about 40 kilometers. There was a big city. And at that time, in German, like the state. Dubno was the name. There was a factory there. And like I mentioned before, I was a cattle dealer. And somehow, I became a buyer for the factory, cattle, because this was my profession. And they didn't take me into the army, the Russians. And then the Germans came in. It didn't take too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you saying that you were a buyer for a factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: A factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Owned by the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not by the Russians, but by the neighbors, farmers-- yeah, pigs. And which the Jewish law didn't allow it. But I did what I had to do. And cows, yes. And that was for the Russians, you know. And then the Germans came in. And when the Germans came in-- because I had fields too. I mentioned this. I had a farm after, and with permit from the-- I had to work on the farm. That's why it wasn't in the city. And I worked on the farm. And then they make already-- they called it at that time-- I'm talking about the Germans, because the Russians left already. And the Germans took it over. And at that time, they start to take to-- you know, work. And I had the farm. And we came from the city a little-- a permit to go on the farm to take off, where I  was. And after-- I can't tell you exactly the amounts-- they start to move us from Murwc and the next city to the ghetto-- started to build a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me approximately when you remember the Germans coming into your area of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: They come in-- May, June-- between May and June. In spring.

speaker: interviewer
text: In spring? Of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year? 1942. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The town you mentioned before, the large town that was near you was Dubno?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the town what I used to live was Mlynow-- M-L-Y-N-O-W, yes. It was there. And my little town from where-- it's an extra kilometer it was almost the same. It was Murwc, but it's not necessary in the history. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell that little town?

speaker: interviewee
text: That little town, Murwc-- M-U-R-W-C. But that's-- it's not exactly the spelling. But I try my best at this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the ghetto was established, did you experience any anti-Jewish aktions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because when they came in, the Germans-- I mentioned there before, we had two little synagogues. And one synagogue, they just came in. And they find they have one-- a deaf Jew. And one, he was, in a  matter of fact, my brother's wife's grandfather-- and he was 90 years, at that time, old-- in the synagogue. And they shoot them, both of them. Right in the-- this was the beginning in this city. We buried them the next day in-- I have still have memory. And I mentioned this. I find from them both-- he was an old man-- blood in the synagogue like from cattle or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who shot him in the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans. The Germans, when they came in. They find him in the synagogue-- what I mentioned, two synagogues and one synagogue-- both of them. One was so deaf he was going from door to door asking for a piece of bread. At that time, poor guy. And he was at that point, he couldn't speak. They both found them in the synagogue, and they shoot him in the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which synagogue was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the-- you need a name from the synagogue? Triska Synagogue-- this was the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the two men who were killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one name only. Not for the deaf one. As a matter of fact, the old man was my brother's wife's grandfather. And he was all his life a builder of houses, they called them, yes. He was working on the farms. And he was 90 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I have pictures for him too. His name was Motle-- M-O-T-L-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was his first name or last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was the first name. The last name was Greenspan. G-R-E-N-S-P-- you know, Maybe it's not exactly the spelling. It's a long time. And in my mind, it's not the same like it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any other anti-Jewish aktions taken by the Germans in your village, in your area, before the ghetto was established?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of tragedies in that time. The first tragedy when they came in-- I didn't tell you before. Anyway, they took us in a line, Jewish people. It was because before they came in, the Germans, they bombed the airport. And they bombed to put in a hole. And at that time-- and I was also in the group where they took us. And at that time, we left on the-- on the [NON-ENGLISH]. Like a pick-up. At that time, we were fanatics. We always thought how this was God's world. And he gave the permit. Eight children-- with only one child with a father and mother-- picked up eight children. And they killed them there on the farm, because they find somewhere a hole. And they made us-- they dig the hole. Anyway, yes. Eight children-- it was the first day when they took us to the airport to fill up where they bombed before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the airport, all of you men were made to fill the holes from the bombs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fill the holes from the bombs. And I was also with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it that you became part of that group of men?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us from the little city.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the house. They came around. This was-- from us to the airport was about three or four kilometers at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the German soldiers came to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to the-- they took-- yes. Germans already. It's a lot of memory. When you start to talk, came a lot of things to the memory, which maybe it's not even necessary, because it will take a long time. And it's not necessary. At that time, they put us in a line, Jews, where they picked us up from the houses to take to the airport. And Germans, one side to the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember thinking because it's maybe, to my way of thinking, not necessary even to mention this. Struck with a key. In aktion, an old man, because he was next to me with a white beard, an old man. And he fell down on me and said, oy, oy, oy-- you know? To me now, I think, maybe it's not necessary to even mention these things. It's impossible to believe things which one human can to do to another human. So you saw the man next to you,  an old man--

speaker: interviewee
text: An old man-- maybe in that time, 60 or 55. But at that time, there was not 80 years old people. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to the man? He was hit with keys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who hit him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, it was Germans. Only Germans. Because they were, they were-- they was in the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they hit him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he had beard, and he was  a Jew. They knew it because they took us from the homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because he had a beard?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they take those eight boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: For no reason. They find out later they had a mistake. They mentioned-- the one where they killed him, mentioned himself was a tractor. A tractor was a machine what's in the field. He went by and he touched the wire. And they came. And they were working at that time, not too far. And they mentioned they did it-- the wire cut-- for no reason. This was even not an explanation. We found out later  from somebody. Maybe it's the right explanation. Maybe not because of what this was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the boy supposedly cut a wire. And that's why they--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in that time between us Jews the explanation. Somebody talked to somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know these boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every one of them, of course. Maybe I can mention the names. Maybe not. Of course, my mind is not even what it was yesterday or to this morning, because it makes me-- when I start to talk about this, still I'm a human. And I'm touchy now more than I used to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember any of those boys' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Names? Yes, I can remember. Moishe, the first one, yes. Borel-- I mean, it's Jewish names.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are all first names?

speaker: interviewee
text: First names. Yeah. The second name maybe I don't even remember some of it, because I was born in this little city.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were these boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: 13, 14 years-- young kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: The old man who was standing next to you, who was beaten and fell--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name? Yes, I can still concentrate in my mind. Two names. I mean, there were two names. A Jewish name I can tell it Chaim Meyer. The first name was Chaim and meyer, because there were two names. And the second name was Teitleman. The reason I remember the second name, because his nephew is in Israel. And I looked in the book. And I find a picture for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction at this time from the non-Jewish local people to the activity against the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to describe exactly. Certain people-- I know some people where they would sigh, [INAUDIBLE] also some of them. And some of them, they were laughing, you know, because they killed Jews. The world is the same. You find even now in the world  certain people where they have prejudice. And-- but at that time, it was more, because the life was a poor life in all Europe in that day. And a Jew was always more, because when I had today-- this I'm describing from my mind only. Because he lived decent. And he didn't spend for-- for what other some nationalities was drinking or this. And he had, like, for our Seder, the custom for Jews was to have white bread. You know, [INAUDIBLE]. You understand this. And this was the jealousy-- not other. It was not a big jealousy between money-wise, millionaires or not millionaires. Always poor. The whole area was poor. Even the Jew was poor too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have some friends who were Gentiles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As a matter of fact, I mentioned to you-- and they deserve to mention him again. The first what I think I mentioned already to you, he was the head from this village. And at that time, it was like over here the mayor. And he went every Friday to the city hall. This was the custom of the Germans, [INAUDIBLE] and they came. Yeah, he was friend a friend of me. He brought me to the ghetto-- whatever. Before I went to the ghetto, I gave him what I had to give him at that time, you know. And he came Friday always. He had a permit, because he was to come to the ghetto. He brought me help, food. As a matter of fact, he risked-- they risked their lives on the worst day when I left the ghetto was a couple nights in there. And he was the head from the city. And they know it. And the lady used to bring me some food. Yes. And when they [INAUDIBLE] and they deserved, I shall mention their names. Even now, in the memory from the world over is going to read. There was all kind of people in every nationality, in the same thing, like here. Every human has a different nature.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will talk more about the ghetto in just a moment. I want to ask you, do you remember what you did when the Germans first came in, in October, 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I told you. When the Germans came in, I had the first weeks-- I had a permit because I had a farm, a little farm too. I don't know you if you heard that. And they gave me a permit to go on the farm, which I was at that time lucky in my mind, I wouldn't be in the city, because the city was not secured with life. They find a Jew, they kill them. They had to do it, not they had to do  it. Some of them, human nature. Some of them, it was a form pleasure. He'd kill somebody-- that's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would the German soldiers come right into your house? Would they come right into your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they came, they came in, not to my-- yes, they came in. As a matter of fact, they came into my mother's house. She was in the first line from the road when they came in. And they came in. And yes, and found my mother, yes. As a  matter of fact, they hit her. But it was nothing new, something when they started to hit some Jewish-- anything, ladies, men, girls, boys. It doesn't mean anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German soldiers hit your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: If they told me to take my mother?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Are you saying that the German soldiers hit your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As a matter of fact, after we came back, she was crying, you know, with some marks on the body, of course. Not only my mother, whoever they found alive. This was their hunting season.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to talk with you now about your experiences in the ghetto. And I'd like to ask you first to tell us the name of the ghetto in your town.

speaker: interviewee
text: The name was the expression-- Mlynow Ghetto. Mlynow was the little city. And ghetto is-- Mlynow Ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, approximately, not exactly, of course, my mind now especially when  I'm a little bit more nervous than any other day of my life. Mlynow is Mlynow Ghetto. They ghetto was-- the little city was the name. Mlynow Ghetto, I mentioned.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how long were you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, approximately-- I told you, you asked me, and I changed the subject. This is my nature. I'm sorry. Maybe about eight, nine months. Not even close to a year, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me approximately when the ghetto was set up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think in June.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there more than one ghetto in your area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only one ghetto in our area.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how big was the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't even describe. Not even half a mile or something, you know. They took it off. And the sites from the little city from Mlynow that I mentioned there. They took it over, a couple side streets. And one synagogue was in-- in the area, an old synagogue. And there was in the middle, there was a big-- like a puddle with water. At that time, it was not like here. Yes, I remember this was also in the little ghetto. I say the little ghetto because not too far was Dubno, the Kreis [INAUDIBLE], the state was a bigger ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Dubno?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-U-B-N-O. Dubno.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about how far away was that from--

speaker: interviewee
text: The nearest city was about 13 or 14 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many people lived in the Mlynow Ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Dubno Ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, in Mlynow Ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Mlynow, approximately from the little two cities, and from the villages, maybe it's a couple thousand-- maybe. Yeah, I can't give you the exact-- which even at that time, they didn't know the statistics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the Dubno ghetto larger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's a bigger city. It was-- like in the time, in Dubno, they called it Kreis Dubno. Like over here, the state, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many people, do you guess, were in that ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can only guess. Maybe 5,000, 6,000 Jews, yes, possibly. A bigger city.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were never in the Dubno Ghetto. You were always in the Mlynow Ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as a matter of fact, [INAUDIBLE]. But I will answer what you ask me. And then I will come to this if you will ask me. You know, in the ghetto, I became also-- not I became-- they called it at that time the Judenrat. And I was from my little city from when they put it in-- the head. And another name, another Jewish name. As a matter of fact, I came to the history, what came to my mind. It was also-- the name was Shlomo Sherman. It was the ghetto from our city, both of us, from our ghetto, the leaders.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and Shlomo Sherman were representatives on the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from our population in the Mlynow Ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you chosen to be in the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I didn't tell you this. My sister-- she survived with me. I told you I had one sister. She's dead now. Last year, she died in Israel.

words.text: I told you I had one sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name was Bunya. As a matter of fact, I have here some pictures from her. And she survived. That's what you asked me, right? She survived with me. And she lived with me in the ghetto in the hole, in the foxhole where I was at night. My life took years. And it's a long history. And I start to talk to you, and you ask me a question-- came another thing in my mind, which I didn't mention at  the time. I don't know if it's necessary or not necessary. But I couldn't say all the details, when-- when I-- when I was hiding already, I came to the-- I don't know if you asked me this. What do you ask me now?

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you selected to be one of the two representatives of the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this I-- yes. Because I told you, I mentioned-- I had already. I was working on my farm. And there was another Jew from our little city. And the Judenrat from our little city to Mlynow because Mlynow was the bigger little city of the two cities. I mentioned its name. And they picked him up. And then somehow somebody there-- I couldn't tell you exactly took him off-- didn't like him. And he was from a bigger city, educated man, intelligent man. They didn't like him. They took it off him. And they picked me. Or somebody gave the name, mine or not. This I don't know. But I had a sister. She survived with me. And she walked by an officer. And she asked an officer, a German officer already-- told him about my brother has a field. And he works in a field. Now, they picked him to go with the Judenrat to take him. She thought she will have some explanation from him. Then he answered here. Your brother was chosen in German is going to-- because he doesn't want to do the job for the Wermacht at the time. Wermacht mean-- and I had a message. And I had to go. And I became a Judenrat in my city. And I went to-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain what you mean by the Wermacht.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Wermacht was the army, the German Army. The Judenrat is from the ghetto-- like you know, an officer. But I don't how to describe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you thought you had no choice. You had to serve in the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course no choice. I didn't want it, because thought this is-- [INAUDIBLE] because I have to go to the city, the little city. And then I will be a candidate to be [INAUDIBLE]. I was better secured on the field, because I was working my field. And that's what I looked for some help, maybe I can find, not to be in the Judenrat. But I had to accept.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you and your family have to go into the ghetto? Why didn't you stay on the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took it away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took it away?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, of course, with the help from the Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The  Ukrainians. Of course, they were-- it was in some of them, every human had a different nature. Some of them, they were good. They'd like to help you. And some of them, they would like to kill you. Maybe they can take away from the shirt from you. You know, it's human nature. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when they took away your farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They took it away from me and somebody else was working. Who knows?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. I had to leave the whole city. They took us away, not only from the farm. They took us away from my home. I still had a home too. But I had to go to the ghetto. This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did some German soldier come to your house and tell you you had to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the Germans themselves didn't come, because the most in our area was Ukrainian. And they became poor. The whole area was the poor area. And they became-- you know, like-- the area was not educated, which I mentioned to you. I didn't have a college degree. And they didn't have even sign their names, a lot of them. And when you haven't got educated, and you have a choice in a poor situation to get something for nothing of this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the Ukrainians who forced you off your land?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course I know a lot of names. As a matter of fact, I don't know if I have still-- I still have a little paper from when I moved to my farm-- from my farm they gave me, because they made me to move somewhere else when the Russians came in, after I was liberated from the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a piece of paper from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's on the piece of paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: How much land they had, and a sample from the city in that time, like over here, the mayor was a-- lord and a mayor. Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much land did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, about seven acres or something. Not 15 acres.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember the names of the Ukrainians who forced you off the land?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a lot of names at that time, because I was a Jew, and they were Ukrainian animals-- you  know, enemies and animals too. Their nature was animals-- some of them, not all of them. They did what they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: This tape is ending. And we'll come right back.
